questions.question: Who wrote the Indian national anthem 'Jana Gana Mana'?
questions.explanation: Jana Gana Mana was written by Rabindranath Tagore in Bengali. It was adopted as the National Anthem of India on 24 January 1950.

questions.question: The Chola Empire was a famous empire from which part of India?
questions.explanation: The Chola Empire was one of the longest-ruling dynasties in South India (Tamil Nadu), known for maritime power, temple architecture, and administration.

questions.question: Who discovered the sea route to India?
questions.explanation: Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama discovered the sea route to India in 1498, arriving at Calicut (Kozhikode) on the Malabar Coast.

questions.question: The Great Wall of China was built primarily to protect against:
questions.explanation: The Great Wall of China was built over centuries, primarily to protect Chinese states and empires against Xiongnu nomadic tribes from the north — later associated with Mongol invasions.

questions.question: Which Chola king sent a naval expedition to Southeast Asia?
questions.explanation: Rajendra Chola I (1014–1044 CE) sent a famous naval expedition to Southeast Asia (Srivijaya Empire in Sumatra and Malay Peninsula) around 1025 CE.

questions.question: The Subsidiary Alliance system was introduced by:
questions.explanation: Lord Wellesley introduced the Subsidiary Alliance system (1798) to bring Indian states under British control without direct annexation. Under this, Indian rulers had to maintain British troops at their cost.

questions.question: The Revolt of 1857 began at:
questions.explanation: The Revolt of 1857 began on 10 May 1857 at Meerut (Uttar Pradesh) when Indian sepoys refused to use the greased cartridges and revolted.

questions.question: Sangam literature belongs to which period of Tamil history?
questions.explanation: Sangam literature belongs to the period approximately 300 BCE to 300 CE. It is the oldest body of Tamil literature, covering love (Akam) and heroic (Puram) themes.

questions.question: Who was the first Indian Governor-General of free India?
questions.explanation: C. Rajagopalachari (Rajaji) was the first and only Indian Governor-General of India (1948-1950), succeeding Lord Mountbatten.

questions.question: The 'Cabinet Mission Plan' (1946) proposed:
options: A Constituent Assembly with separate groups for Hindu-majority and Muslim-majority provinces
A federal structure with provinces and a centre
questions.explanation: The Cabinet Mission Plan (1946) proposed a three-tier federal structure: a Union of India at the top, groups of provinces in the middle, and individual provinces at the bottom. It aimed to keep India united while giving autonomy to Muslim-majority areas.

questions.question: The 'Hunter Commission' (1882) recommended:
questions.explanation: The Hunter Commission (1882) under William Hunter reviewed the state of education in British India. It recommended the strengthening of primary and secondary education.

questions.question: The earliest known humans in India belong to the:
questions.explanation: The Palaeolithic (Old Stone Age) period (500,000–10,000 BCE) contains the earliest evidence of human habitation in India. Stone tools found at Soan valley and Pallavaram (near Chennai).

questions.question: The Palaeolithic site 'Attirampakkam' is located near which city?
questions.explanation: Attirampakkam is a Palaeolithic archaeological site near Chennai (formerly Madras), Tamil Nadu. Recent studies show stone tools here date back ~1.5 million years — among the oldest in India.

questions.question: Neolithic settlements in South India are found mainly in:
options: River valleys of the Gangetic plain
The Deccan Plateau (Karnataka, AP, Tamil Nadu)
questions.explanation: Neolithic cultures in South India (c. 3000–1000 BCE) are found on the Deccan Plateau — sites like Brahmagiri (Karnataka), Piklihal, Utnur, Maski, Hallur. They used stone axes and kept cattle.

questions.question: The Bhagavad Gita is a part of which epic?
questions.explanation: The Bhagavad Gita is in the Bhishma Parva (chapters 23-40) of the Mahabharata. It contains the dialogue between Krishna and Arjuna on the Kurukshetra battlefield. It is often called the 'Song of God'.

questions.question: The 'Four Noble Truths' (Chatur Arya Satya) of Buddhism state:
options: The world is eternal and perfect
Life involves suffering (dukkha); suffering has causes (desire); suffering can end; there is a path to end it (Noble Eightfold Path)
Meditation is the only path to liberation
questions.explanation: The Four Noble Truths: 1) Dukkha (life is suffering), 2) Samudaya (suffering arises from desire/craving — tanha), 3) Nirodha (suffering can be ended), 4) Magga (the Noble Eightfold Path leads to liberation).

questions.question: Hinayana and Mahayana are two branches of Buddhism. Mahayana believes:
options: Both monks and laypeople can achieve salvation; the Bodhisattva ideal — staying in the world to help others attain liberation
Strict individual meditation alone leads to liberation
The Buddha was only a human teacher
questions.explanation: Mahayana ('Greater Vehicle'): emphasises Bodhisattva ideal — beings who delay their own Nirvana to save others. Spread to China, Japan, Korea, Tibet. Hinayana ('Lesser Vehicle', now called Theravada) spread to Sri Lanka, Burma, Thailand.

questions.question: The greatest Kushana ruler who patronised Buddhism was:
questions.explanation: Kanishka I (c. 127–150 CE) was the greatest Kushana emperor. He convened the 4th Buddhist Council in Kashmir, patronised Mahayana Buddhism, and his empire stretched from Central Asia to Pataliputra.

questions.question: The Satavahana dynasty ruled in:
options: South India — Deccan region (Andhra Pradesh, Maharashtra, Telangana)
questions.explanation: The Satavahanas (c. 230 BCE–220 CE) ruled the Deccan. Capital at Pratishthana (Paithan, Maharashtra). They were the first great rulers of the Deccan, building the Amravati stupa.

questions.question: The first metal to be used by humans was:
questions.explanation: Copper was the first metal used by humans, followed by Bronze (alloy of copper and tin). The Bronze Age succeeded the Copper Age (Chalcolithic Age).

questions.question: The word 'Arya' in Sanskrit means:
questions.explanation: 'Arya' in Sanskrit means noble, honorable, or respectable. It was a cultural/social term, not a racial one.

questions.question: The Pali Canon (Tripitaka) consists of:
questions.explanation: Pali Canon (Tripitaka = Three Baskets): Vinaya Pitaka (monastic rules), Sutta Pitaka (discourses), Abhidhamma Pitaka (philosophical analysis).

questions.question: The doctrine of 'Bodhisattva' is central to:
questions.explanation: Bodhisattva (enlightenment being) concept is central to Mahayana Buddhism — beings who delay their own nirvana to help all sentient beings attain liberation.

questions.question: The Kanishka was the greatest ruler of the:
questions.explanation: Kanishka I was the greatest ruler of the Kushan Empire (1st-2nd century CE), patronized Buddhism, convened the 4th Buddhist Council at Kundalvana (Kashmir).

questions.question: The Sangam age in Tamil Nadu is generally dated:
questions.explanation: The Sangam Age (300 BCE to 300 CE) is the early classical period of Tamil civilization known from Sangam literature — a corpus of poetry by hundreds of Tamil poets.

questions.question: The Three Sangams (literary academies) were presided over by the kings of:
questions.explanation: According to later Tamil tradition, the three legendary Sangams were patronized by Pandya kings at Madurai (1st & 3rd) and Kapatapuram (2nd).

questions.question: 'Tolkappiyam' is a grammatical treatise on:
questions.explanation: Tolkappiyam by Tolkappiyar is the oldest surviving work in Tamil literature — a comprehensive grammar covering phonology, word formation, and poetics. Part of early Sangam literature.

questions.question: 'Ettuthokai' means:
questions.explanation: Ettuthokai (Eight Anthologies) is a collection of 8 Sangam-age anthologies of Tamil poetry: Ainkurunuru, Narrinai, Akananuru, Purananuru, Kalittokai, Kuruntokai, Patirruppattu, and Paripatal.

questions.question: 'Pattupattu' refers to:
questions.explanation: Pattupattu (Ten Idylls) are 10 longer Sangam poems, distinct from the Eight Anthologies, covering topics from love to war to nature.

questions.question: The Chera king famous in Sangam literature for feeding both sides in the Kurukshetra war is:
questions.explanation: Udiyanjeral (Perum Cheral Irumporai) is the Chera king praised in Sangam literature (Purananuru) for feeding both the armies of the Mahabharata war.

questions.question: The theme of 'Manimekalai' is:
questions.explanation: Manimekalai by Sittalai Sattanar propagates Buddhist philosophy. The heroine Manimekalai becomes a Buddhist nun and practices compassion and charity.

questions.question: The Tirukkural is divided into three sections called:
questions.explanation: Tirukkural by Tiruvalluvar is divided into: Aram (Virtue/Dharma), Porul (Wealth/Polity), Inbam (Love/Pleasure) — 133 chapters, 1330 couplets.

questions.question: Which Sangam text is known as 'Tamil Bible'?
questions.explanation: Tirukkural by Tiruvalluvar is often called 'Tamil Bible' or 'Universal Veda' for its universal wisdom on virtue, wealth, and love applicable to all humanity.

questions.question: Who authored 'Ashtadhyayi'?
questions.explanation: Panini's Ashtadhyayi (4000 sutras) is the foundational text of Sanskrit grammar, one of the greatest intellectual achievements of ancient civilization.

questions.question: The founder of the Pala dynasty of Bengal was:
questions.explanation: Gopala (750-770 CE) founded the Pala dynasty in Bengal. The Palas were great patrons of Buddhism and built the famous Vikramashila University.

questions.question: Charaka Samhita deals with:
questions.explanation: Charaka Samhita by Charaka is one of the foundational texts of Ayurveda (Indian medicine) covering internal medicine, surgery, pharmacology, and medical ethics.

questions.question: The Ajanta Caves are famous for:
questions.explanation: Ajanta Caves (Maharashtra, 2nd century BCE – 7th century CE) are UNESCO World Heritage Sites famous for their magnificent Buddhist cave paintings and sculptures depicting Jataka stories.

questions.question: The famous astronomer who stated Earth rotates on its axis was:
questions.explanation: Aryabhata (476-550 CE) stated that the Earth rotates on its own axis, causing apparent movement of stars — a revolutionary concept 1000 years before Copernicus.

questions.question: The 'Periplus of the Erythraean Sea' was written by:
questions.explanation: 'Periplus of the Erythraean Sea' (1st century CE) written by an anonymous Greek merchant describes ancient trade routes and ports along the Indian Ocean, including Indian ports.

questions.question: The Satavahana dynasty ruled mainly over:
questions.explanation: Satavahanas (1st century BCE – 3rd century CE) were the first major dynasty of the Deccan, ruling from Pratishthana (Paithan). They were great patrons of Buddhism and trade.

questions.question: Sangam Age Tamil literature mentions foreign trade with:
questions.explanation: Sangam literature mentions extensive trade with Rome and Greece — export of spices, pearls, cotton; import of wine, gold, horses. Roman coins found at Arikamedu and other Tamil sites.

questions.question: The Rashtrakutas were the successors of:
questions.explanation: Dantidurga overthrew the Chalukya ruler Kirtivarman II and established the Rashtrakuta dynasty in 753 CE, making them the major power in the Deccan.

questions.question: 'Kamban Ramayanam' (Tamil Ramayana) was written during the reign of:
questions.explanation: Kambar wrote the Tamil Ramayana (Iramavataram) around 12th century CE during the later Chola period, supported by a local chieftain Sadaiyappa Vallal.

questions.question: The Pandya dynasty's capital was:
questions.explanation: Madurai was the capital of the Pandya dynasty throughout history — from Sangam Age through medieval period. Ancient Madurai is described in detail in Sangam literature (Maturaikkanci).

questions.question: The Meenakshi Amman Temple at Madurai was mainly built by:
questions.explanation: The Meenakshi Amman Temple in its present form was largely built during Tirumalai Nayak's rule (1623-1659 CE), though the temple itself dates back to much earlier Pandya times.

questions.question: The Chera ports mentioned in Sangam literature include:
questions.explanation: Musiri (Muziris) and Thondi were important Chera ports mentioned in Sangam literature and Periplus, known for trade in spices, particularly black pepper with Rome.

questions.question: The 'Darasuram Temple' (Airavatesvara) was built by:
questions.explanation: Airavatesvara Temple at Darasuram (near Kumbakonam) was built by Rajaraja II in the 12th century CE. Along with Thanjavur and Gangaikonda Cholapuram, it's a UNESCO heritage site (Great Living Chola Temples).

questions.question: Guru Nanak Dev founded:
questions.explanation: Guru Nanak Dev (1469-1539 CE) founded Sikhism, emphasizing one God, equality of all humans, truthful living, service to humanity, and opposition to caste discrimination.

questions.question: The Chishti Silsila of Sufi order in India was founded by:
questions.explanation: Moinuddin Chishti (1142-1236 CE) brought the Chishti order to India, establishing his khanqah at Ajmer. He and his followers emphasized love of God and service.

questions.question: Ramanujacharya's Vishishtadvaita philosophy is the basis of:
questions.explanation: Ramanuja's Vishishtadvaita is the philosophical basis of Sri Vaishnavism, which combines the philosophical tradition with the devotional tradition of the Alvars.

questions.question: Tukaram's compositions are called:
questions.explanation: Tukaram's compositions are called Abhangas — devotional poems addressed to Lord Vitthal (Vishnu) of Pandharpur. They are in Marathi and form part of the Varkari tradition.

questions.question: Which Bhakti saint from Tamil Nadu was born in a weaver community?
questions.explanation: Tiruvalluvar, author of Tirukkural, is traditionally believed to have been born in a weaver (Paraiyar or Valluvar) community, though this is debated.

questions.question: The Wood's Education Despatch (1854) is considered:
questions.explanation: Wood's Education Despatch (1854) by Charles Wood is called the 'Magna Carta of Indian Education' — it introduced English as medium of instruction, established universities, and created a department of education.

questions.question: The first railway line in India ran between:
questions.explanation: India's first railway line ran between Bombay (Bori Bunder) and Thane on 16 April 1853, a distance of 34 km. The train pulled by three engines carried 400 passengers.

questions.question: The Simon Commission (1927) was boycotted because:
questions.explanation: The Simon Commission (1927) was boycotted by all Indian political parties as it had no Indian members — all 7 members were British MPs. 'Simon Go Back' became the protest slogan.

questions.question: Who gave the slogan 'Jai Hind'?
questions.explanation: 'Jai Hind' (Victory to India) was given as a rallying cry by Subhas Chandra Bose, head of the Indian National Army (INA). It became India's national salutation.

questions.question: Mangal Pandey was a sepoy in:
questions.explanation: Mangal Pandey was a sepoy in the 34th Bengal Native Infantry who fired at a British sergeant-major on 29 March 1857, becoming the first martyr of the 1857 revolt.

questions.question: The Ghadar Party was founded by:
questions.explanation: Ghadar Party was founded in 1913 at San Francisco by Sohan Singh Bhakna (president) and Lala Hardayal (Secretary) among Indian immigrants to organize a mutiny against British rule.

questions.question: Chandrashekhar Azad died at:
questions.explanation: Chandrashekhar Azad died at Alfred Park (now Chandrashekhar Azad Park), Allahabad on 27 February 1931, when surrounded by police — he shot himself to avoid capture, living up to his vow never to be taken alive.

questions.question: Lala Lajpat Rai died after police lathi charge during protests against:
questions.explanation: Lala Lajpat Rai (Punjab Kesari) died on 17 November 1928 due to injuries sustained during police lathi charge on protesters against the Simon Commission in Lahore.

questions.question: Aurobindo Ghosh was associated with:
questions.explanation: Sri Aurobindo Ghosh was an extremist nationalist, editor of 'Yugantar', involved in Alipore Bomb Case (1908). After release, moved to Pondicherry where he founded his spiritual ashram.

questions.question: The Government of India Act 1935 introduced:
options: Provincial autonomy and dyarchy at center
questions.explanation: Government of India Act 1935 introduced provincial autonomy (provinces got self-government), dyarchy at the Center, and established the framework for a federal structure — largely formed the basis of India's Constitution.

questions.question: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar was the Chairman of:
questions.explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar chaired the Drafting Committee of the Constituent Assembly (1947-1949), drafting India's Constitution. He is called 'Father of the Indian Constitution'.

questions.question: The Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel was instrumental in:
questions.explanation: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel (Iron Man of India), as Deputy PM and Home Minister, integrated 562 princely states into the Indian Union through diplomacy and force (1947-50).

questions.question: The American Civil War was fought between:
questions.explanation: American Civil War (1861-1865) between the Northern Union states and Southern Confederate states, primarily over slavery. Union victory abolished slavery through the 13th Amendment (1865).

questions.question: The Lingayat movement was founded by:
questions.explanation: Basavanna (12th century CE) founded the Lingayat (Veerashaiva) movement in Karnataka, challenging the caste system and Brahminical rituals, emphasizing devotion to Shiva.

questions.question: Chaitanya Mahaprabhu spread which form of Vaishnavism?
questions.explanation: Chaitanya Mahaprabhu (1486-1534 CE) spread Gaudiya Vaishnavism (Bengal Vaishnavism) through kirtan and sankirtana, emphasizing extreme devotion to Krishna.

questions.question: Saint Raidas (Ravidas) was a disciple of:
questions.explanation: Raidas (Ravidas, 15th century) was a disciple of Ramananda and a contemporary of Kabir. He was from the chamaar (cobbler) community — challenged caste system through his devotional poetry.

questions.question: The Indian National Army trials (INA trials, 1945) were held at:
questions.explanation: The INA trials were held at Red Fort, Delhi (November 1945), charging INA officers with treason against the British. The trials created massive public outrage that unified Indians.

questions.question: The Theosophical Society in India was established at:
questions.explanation: Theosophical Society was founded in New York (1875) by H.P. Blavatsky and H.S. Olcott. Its headquarters was established at Adyar, Madras (Chennai) in 1882.

questions.question: Annie Besant founded the Home Rule League in:
questions.explanation: Annie Besant established the Home Rule League in September 1916, demanding self-government for India within the British Empire. Bal Gangadhar Tilak set up a parallel league in April 1916.

questions.question: Ramakrishna Mission was founded by:
questions.explanation: Ramakrishna Mission was founded by Swami Vivekananda in 1897 at Belur Math, near Calcutta. It is dedicated to the teachings of Ramakrishna Paramhansa and humanitarian service.

questions.question: Swami Vivekananda represented India at the Parliament of World Religions in:
questions.explanation: Swami Vivekananda addressed the Parliament of World Religions in Chicago on 11 September 1893, his famous opening 'Sisters and Brothers of America' electrifying the audience.

questions.question: Tirunelveli is associated with which major uprising against British rule?
questions.explanation: Veerapandiya Kattabomman of Panchalamkurichi revolted against British taxation in 1799. He was captured and hanged at Kayathar. He is a legendary Tamil folk hero.

questions.question: Who was known as 'Deshiya Kavi' (National Poet) of Tamil Nadu?
questions.explanation: Subramania Bharati (1882-1921) is called 'Mahakavi' and 'National Poet' of Tamil Nadu for his patriotic poetry. His songs inspired the freedom movement and are still sung widely.

questions.question: The Maraimalai Adigal contributed to:
questions.explanation: Maraimalai Adigal (1876-1950) was the father of the Thanith Tamil (Pure Tamil) movement, working to remove Sanskrit loan words from Tamil and restore its classical purity.

questions.question: Who became the first Indian to be the Governor-General of a dominion?
questions.explanation: C. Rajagopalachari (Rajaji) became the first (and only) Indian Governor-General of India (1948-1950), taking over from Lord Mountbatten. Earlier was the last Governor-General of British India.

questions.question: The 'Drain Theory' was propounded by:
questions.explanation: Dadabhai Naoroji ('Grand Old Man of India') propounded the Drain of Wealth theory — that British rule drained India's wealth to Britain through trade, taxation, and services. Wrote 'Poverty and Un-British Rule in India'.

questions.question: The Kushana ruler who adopted Buddhism was:
questions.explanation: Kanishka I was the most famous Kushana ruler (c. 127-150 CE) who adopted Buddhism, convened the 4th Buddhist Council, and spread Buddhism across Central Asia.

questions.question: The Pala dynasty was known for patronizing:
questions.explanation: The Pala rulers of Bengal (8th-12th centuries) were great patrons of Buddhism and Tantric art. They built Vikramashila University and sent Buddhist missionaries to Tibet.

questions.question: Operation Polo (1948) was related to:
questions.explanation: Operation Polo (September 1948): Indian military action (police action) to integrate the princely state of Hyderabad into India after Nizam Osman Ali Khan refused to accede.

questions.question: Which freedom fighter was called 'Punjab Kesari'?
questions.explanation: Lala Lajpat Rai was called 'Punjab Kesari' (Lion of Punjab) for his fiery nationalism. He was also called 'Sher-e-Punjab'. He died after police lathi charge during Simon Commission protests.

questions.question: Indian National Army's women's regiment was named:
questions.explanation: INA's women's regiment was the Rani of Jhansi Regiment, named after Rani Lakshmi Bai, commanded by Captain Lakshmi Sahgal (Dr. Lakshmi). About 1,500 women enlisted.

questions.question: Who sang 'Saare Jahan Se Achha' (1904)?
questions.explanation: 'Saare Jahan Se Achha' was written by Muhammad Iqbal in 1904. Ironically, Iqbal later became a key proponent of a separate Muslim state (Pakistan).

questions.question: 'Bande Mataram' was composed by:
questions.explanation: Bande Mataram was composed by Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay in 1870s, first appearing in his novel Anandamath (1882). It became the rallying song of the independence movement.

questions.question: The Attlee Declaration (February 1947) announced:
questions.explanation: British PM Clement Attlee's Declaration (February 1947) announced transfer of power to Indian hands by June 1948 — accelerated by Mountbatten who chose August 1947.

questions.question: Pattali Makkal Katchi (PMK) was founded by:
questions.explanation: PMK was founded by Dr. S. Ramadoss in 1989 in Tamil Nadu, representing primarily the Vanniyar community. It emerged as a significant regional political force in Tamil Nadu.

questions.question: The Chola king who brought the Ganga water to his capital was:
questions.explanation: Rajendra I (Gangaikonda Chola) brought Ganga water (symbolic) from his northern campaign to his new capital Gangaikonda Cholapuram, cementing his title as the 'Chola who conquered the Ganga'.

questions.question: Tiruvalluvar Day is celebrated on:
questions.explanation: Tiruvalluvar Day (Tiruvalluvar Dhinam) is celebrated on 15th or 16th January (coinciding with Pongal/Thai 2nd day) in honour of the Tamil poet-philosopher Tiruvalluvar.

questions.question: The founder of the Sikh religion is:
questions.explanation: Guru Nanak Dev (1469-1539) founded Sikhism, the world's fifth-largest religion. He emphasized Ik Onkar (One God), equality, service (seva), and truthful living.

questions.question: The 10th and last Sikh Guru who founded the Khalsa:
questions.explanation: Guru Gobind Singh (1666-1708), the 10th Sikh Guru, founded the Khalsa (Pure) in 1699, giving Sikhs the 5 Ks — Kesh, Kara, Kachera, Kirpan, Kangha. He declared Guru Granth Sahib as the eternal Guru.

questions.question: The 'Ryotwari system' of land revenue was introduced in:
questions.explanation: Ryotwari system (introduced by Thomas Munro and Alexander Read in Madras Presidency, early 19th century) fixed revenue directly with individual cultivators (ryots), bypassing zamindars.

questions.question: The Mahalwari system was mainly introduced in:
questions.explanation: Mahalwari system (introduced by Holt Mackenzie, 1822) fixed land revenue with the village community (mahal) collectively in North-Western Provinces (UP) and Punjab.

questions.question: The three Sangam kingdoms (Muvendars) of ancient Tamil Nadu were:
questions.explanation: The three Muvendars (great kings) of the Sangam Age were: Cheras (capital: Vanji/Karur), Cholas (capital: Puhar/Kaveripattinam), and Pandyas (capital: Madurai). They ruled Tamil Nadu from c. 300 BCE to 300 CE.

questions.question: The Sangam literature was compiled in three Sangams (assemblies). Where was the Third Sangam held?
questions.explanation: Third Sangam: Uttara Madurai (present-day Madurai). It produced surviving Sangam literature: Ettuthokai, Patthupattu, Pathinenkeezhkanakku. The first two Sangams (Kapatapuram, Alai) are legendary/submerged.

questions.question: The Tolkappiyam, the oldest Tamil grammar work, was compiled by:
questions.explanation: Tolkappiyam was compiled by Tolkappiyar during the Third Sangam. It is the oldest surviving Tamil grammar text, covering phonology (Ezhutthiyal), words (Sollliyal), and meaning (Porulliyal).

questions.question: The Tirukkural was written by:
questions.explanation: The Tirukkural (Sacred Couplets) was written by Thiruvalluvar. It has 1330 couplets (kurals) in 133 chapters covering Aram (virtue), Porul (wealth), and Inbam (love). It is translated into 100+ languages.

questions.question: The epic 'Silappadikaram' was written by:
questions.explanation: Silappadikaram (The Story of the Anklet) was written by Ilango Adigal, believed to be the brother of Chera king Senguttuvan. It tells the story of Kovalan and Kannagi. It is one of the five great Tamil epics.

questions.question: The epic 'Manimekalai' was written by:
questions.explanation: Manimekalai was written by Sithalai Sattanar (a Buddhist poet). It is the sequel to Silappadikaram, dealing with the adventures of Kannagi's granddaughter Manimekalai, who becomes a Buddhist nun.

questions.question: The Chera kingdom's capital was:
questions.explanation: The Chera kingdom had its capital at Vanji (identified with Karur or Muziri). Their main port was Musiri/Muziri on the Malabar coast — a major Roman trade hub. Cheras controlled Kerala and western Tamil Nadu.

questions.question: The famous Chola king of the Sangam age who completed the Red Canal (Kallanai/Grand Anicut) was:
questions.explanation: Karikalan (Karikala Chola, c. 1st–2nd century CE) was the most famous Sangam Chola. He built the Grand Anicut (Kallanai) across the Kaveri river — the oldest surviving dam in the world (still in use today).

questions.question: The Grand Anicut (Kallanai) built by Karikalan is located on the river:
questions.explanation: The Grand Anicut (Kallanai) is built across the River Kaveri near Trichy (Tiruchirappalli). Built c. 2nd century CE, it is one of the oldest water diversion structures in the world still in use.

questions.question: The Sangam literature is classified into 'Akam' and 'Puram' poetry. Akam deals with:
options: Love and emotions (inner life — classified by five thinais/landscapes)
questions.explanation: Sangam literature: Akam (inner) = love poetry, classified by five thinais (landscapes): Kurinji (mountains-union), Mullai (forest-patient waiting), Marutam (farmland-infidelity), Neytal (sea-separation), Palai (wasteland-elopement). Puram = outer life, heroism, war, generosity.

questions.question: The Roman trade with the Sangam period Tamil kingdoms is evidenced by:
options: Roman coins found in Tamil Nadu sites
questions.explanation: Roman gold coins (many dating to Augustus, Tiberius) found at Vellalur (near Coimbatore), Karur, Arikamedu (near Puducherry, a Roman trading post), and other Tamil sites confirm extensive Roman-Tamil trade.

questions.question: The Sangam-age port of Arikamedu (near Pondicherry) was excavated by:
questions.explanation: Arikamedu (ancient Poduke/Podouke) was excavated by Sir Mortimer Wheeler in 1945. It revealed a major Roman trading station with Arretine pottery, amphorae, glass, and gems — confirming active Indo-Roman trade.

questions.question: The Pallava dynasty had its capital at:
questions.explanation: The Pallava dynasty (3rd–9th century CE) had its capital at Kanchipuram (Kanchi), also called the 'City of Thousand Temples'. Kanchi was a major centre of learning, trade, and religion.

questions.question: The Pallava 'Pancha Rathas' (Five Chariots) at Mahabalipuram are:
options: Five temples built for five Pandava brothers and Draupadi — monolithic rock-cut structures
Stone chariots imported from North India
questions.explanation: The Pancha Rathas (Arjuna, Bhima, Dharmaraja/Yudhishthira, Nakula-Sahadeva, Draupadi Rathas) at Mahabalipuram are monolithic rock-cut structures carved from a single rock. They are incomplete and were never used as temples.

questions.question: The Pallava dynasty was ended by:
questions.explanation: The Pallava dynasty ended when Chola king Aditya I (871–907 CE) defeated and killed the last Pallava king Aparajita Varman c. 897 CE, ending the Pallava dynasty and marking the rise of the Imperial Cholas.

questions.question: The Medieval Chola Empire was founded by:
questions.explanation: Vijayalaya Chola (c. 848 CE) founded the Medieval Chola dynasty by capturing Thanjavur from the Muttaraiyar (who were Pallava vassals). He built the first Nishumbhasudani temple at Thanjavur.

questions.question: The greatest Chola emperor who built the Brihadeeswara (Peruvudaiyar) Temple at Thanjavur was:
questions.explanation: Rajaraja I (985–1014 CE) built the Brihadeeswara Temple (Peruvudaiyar Kovil) at Thanjavur — a masterpiece of Dravidian architecture (UNESCO World Heritage Site). He also built the Brihadeeswarar temple at Gangaikondacholapuram.

questions.question: Rajendra Chola I was famous for:
options: Leading a military expedition to Southeast Asia and North India — reaching the Ganga; titled 'Gangaikonda'
questions.explanation: Rajendra I (1014–1044 CE) sent the Chola navy to Southeast Asia (Sri Vijaya, Kadaram/Malaysia, Indonesia) and his army north to bring Ganga waters — earning the title 'Gangaikonda Cholaperumal'. He built Gangaikondacholapuram as his capital.

questions.question: The Chola administrative unit 'Nadu' was governed by:
options: A viceroy appointed by the king
questions.explanation: Chola administration: Empire → Mandalams (provinces) → Valanadus → Nadus → Urs (villages). Each Nadu was governed by Nattar (assembly of local landowners). The Cholas had a sophisticated system of village self-governance.

questions.question: The Chola local self-government included three types of assemblies. For Brahmin villages, it was called:
questions.explanation: Chola village assemblies: Sabha (for Brahmadeya/agrahara villages of Brahmin settlements), Ur (for non-Brahmin villages), Nagaram (for merchant settlements). Sabha had elected committees (variyams) — example from Uttaramerur inscription.

questions.question: Kulottunga I was a Chola king who also belonged to the:
options: Eastern Chalukya lineage — he united Chola and Vengi kingdoms
questions.explanation: Kulottunga I (1070–1120 CE) was the son of Eastern Chalukya prince Rajendra and Chola princess Ammanga. He united the Chola empire with the Eastern Chalukya kingdom of Vengi, beginning the later Chola dynasty.

questions.question: The Chola navy's maritime expedition to Southeast Asia is called:
options: The Sri Vijaya expedition (Kadaram Expedition, 1025 CE)
questions.explanation: Rajendra Chola I's 1025 CE naval expedition conquered Sri Vijaya (Sumatra) and other Malay kingdoms. This expedition to 'Kadaram' (Kedah) showed the Chola navy's unmatched power. Chola commercial interests in Southeast Asian trade drove this expedition.

questions.question: The bronze sculptures of the Chola period, particularly Nataraja, were cast using:
questions.explanation: Chola bronzes used the lost-wax casting technique: a wax model is made, coated with clay, heated (wax melts/lost), molten bronze is poured in, clay broken to reveal the bronze sculpture. Chola Nataraja bronzes are celebrated worldwide.

questions.question: Muhammad bin Tughlaq's controversial decision to shift the capital from Delhi to Daulatabad (Devagiri) was:
options: A disaster — he forced the entire population of Delhi to relocate, causing mass deaths; later reversed
Done at the request of Sufi saints
questions.explanation: Muhammad bin Tughlaq (1325–1351) shifted the capital from Delhi to Daulatabad (1327) to better control the Deccan. The forced migration caused suffering and deaths. The plan failed — he had to shift back. Ibn Battuta documented this.

questions.question: The capital of the early Chola kings during Sangam age was:
questions.explanation: Uraiyur (near Tiruchirappalli) was the early Chola capital. Kaveripattanam (Puhar/Poompuhar) was their port city.

questions.question: The ancient Roman settlement found in Tamil Nadu is:
questions.explanation: Arikamedu (near Puducherry) was an ancient Roman trading settlement (1st century BCE - 2nd century CE) where Roman pottery, amphorae, and glass were found.

questions.question: The Pallava dynasty was founded by:
questions.explanation: Simhavishnu (575–600 CE) is considered the founder of the Pallava dynasty. He defeated the Kalabhras and established Pallava power in Tamil Nadu.

questions.question: 'Pallava Grantha' script is an ancestor of:
questions.explanation: Pallava Grantha script influenced the development of many Southeast Asian scripts including Thai, Khmer, Burmese, and Javanese, spread through trade and religion.

questions.question: 'Narasimhavarman I' was also known as:
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman I (630-668 CE) was called Mamalla (Great Wrestler) for his wrestling skills. The port city Mamallapuram (Mahabalipuram) is named after him.

questions.question: The Pallavas defeated the Chalukyas in the battle of:
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman I defeated and killed the Chalukya king Pulakesi II and even captured Vatapi (Badami), the Chalukya capital, in 642 CE — hence his title 'Vatapikonda'.

questions.question: The Chola king who completed the construction of Brihadisvara Temple at Thanjavur:
questions.explanation: Rajaraja Chola I (985-1014 CE) built the magnificent Brihadisvara (Peruvudaiyar Kovil) Temple at Thanjavur, completed in 1010 CE. Also called Rajarajesvaram.

questions.question: Rajendra I's campaign to North India is commemorated by:
questions.explanation: Rajendra I (1014-1044 CE) launched an expedition to the Ganges and built a new capital Gangaikonda Cholapuram with its magnificent Gangaikondacholisvara temple.

questions.question: The Chola naval expedition against the Srivijaya Empire (Southeast Asia) was conducted by:
questions.explanation: Rajendra I led a successful naval expedition (1025 CE) against Srivijaya (Malay Peninsula and Sumatra), making Cholas the dominant naval power in Southeast Asia.

questions.question: The Chola bronzes (Nataraja) are made using which technique?
questions.explanation: Chola bronzes use the lost-wax (cire perdue) casting method — a wax model is made, encased in clay, wax is melted out and replaced with molten bronze.

questions.question: The Nataraja (dancing Shiva) bronze sculpture was perfected during:
questions.explanation: The iconic Nataraja bronze sculpture reached its artistic peak during the Chola period (9th-13th century CE). These images became the universal symbol of cosmic dance.

questions.question: The Ur (village assembly) and Nadu (district assembly) in Chola period served as:
questions.explanation: The Chola period had an elaborate local self-government system with Ur (village assembly), Nadu (district-level body), and Mahasabha (elite brahmin assembly in brahmadeya villages).

questions.question: 'Vetti' in Chola inscriptions refers to:
questions.explanation: Vetti (also veth/begari) was forced labor extracted by the state in Chola period, distinct from regular taxation. Villagers had to render free service to the king.

questions.question: Muhammad bin Tughluq's transfer of capital from Delhi to Daulatabad was:
questions.explanation: Muhammad bin Tughluq (1327 CE) transferred the capital from Delhi to Daulatabad (Devagiri) in the Deccan for better control of the empire, but it failed — the northern areas became unmanageable.

questions.question: Ibn Battuta visited India during the reign of:
questions.explanation: Ibn Battuta (Moroccan traveler) visited India 1333-1347 CE during the reign of Muhammad bin Tughluq, who appointed him as Qazi of Delhi. His book Rihla describes India in detail.

questions.question: Ramanuja was the founder of:
questions.explanation: Ramanuja (1017-1137 CE) propounded Vishishtadvaita (qualified non-dualism) — Brahman exists but is characterized by the world and souls as attributes. He emphasized bhakti.

questions.question: Which Bhakti saint was associated with Pandharpur?
questions.explanation: Tukaram (1577-1650 CE) was a Varkari saint associated with Pandharpur (Maharashtra). He composed thousands of abhangas (devotional poems) in Marathi.

questions.question: Kabir was a disciple of:
questions.explanation: Kabir (15th century) was a disciple of Ramananda. His teachings blended Hinduism and Islam, rejecting caste distinctions and religious formalism.

questions.question: The Nayanmars and Alvars were devotees of:
questions.explanation: Nayanmars (63 Tamil Shaiva saints) were devotees of Shiva. Alvars (12 Tamil Vaishnava saints) were devotees of Vishnu. Both composed devotional poetry (6th-9th centuries).

questions.question: Manikkavacakar composed:
questions.explanation: Manikkavacakar (9th century CE) composed Tiruvasagam, one of the most profound devotional texts in Tamil Shaivism, consisting of 51 hymns.

questions.question: The Alvars composed the 'Nalayira Divya Prabandham'. Who compiled it?
questions.explanation: Nathamuni (10th century CE) compiled and systematized the 4000 Tamil Vaishnava hymns (Nalayira Divya Prabandham) composed by the 12 Alvars.

questions.question: Mirabai (Meera) was a devotee of:
questions.explanation: Mirabai (16th century Rajput princess) was an ardent devotee of Lord Krishna. She composed beautiful bhajans in Rajasthani/Braj Bhasha expressing her love for Krishna.

questions.question: The 1857 revolt was called 'First War of Indian Independence' by:
questions.explanation: V.D. Savarkar in his book '1857: The Indian War of Independence' (1909) called the 1857 revolt the First War of Independence, as it was organized resistance to British rule.

questions.question: Who was the leader of Chinese Revolution (1949)?
questions.explanation: Mao Zedong led the Communist Party of China to victory in the Chinese Civil War, proclaiming the People's Republic of China on 1 October 1949.

questions.question: Apartheid policy in South Africa was officially ended in:
questions.explanation: Apartheid officially ended in 1994 with South Africa's first fully democratic elections, which Nelson Mandela won. The formal legislative dismantling of apartheid laws began in 1990-91.

questions.question: The Korean War ended with:
questions.explanation: The Korean War (1950-53) ended with an armistice (ceasefire) on 27 July 1953, technically leaving North and South Korea still at war. The DMZ (demilitarized zone) divides the peninsula.

questions.question: Cuba Crisis (1962) was between:
questions.explanation: Cuban Missile Crisis (October 1962): 13-day confrontation between USA and USSR over Soviet missiles in Cuba. The closest the Cold War came to full-scale nuclear war, resolved diplomatically.

questions.question: The Suez Canal was nationalized by:
questions.explanation: Egyptian President Gamal Abdel Nasser nationalized the Suez Canal in 1956, leading to the Suez Crisis when Britain, France, and Israel invaded Egypt.

questions.question: Which country was first to land humans on the Moon?
questions.explanation: USA's Apollo 11 mission (July 1969) was the first crewed lunar landing. Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin landed on the Moon on 20 July 1969. Armstrong was the first human to walk on the Moon.

questions.question: The First World War was also known as:
questions.explanation: World War I (1914-1918) was contemporaneously called 'The Great War' or 'The War to End All Wars'. The name 'World War I' became common only after the Second World War began.

questions.question: The Holocaust was the genocide of:
options: Jews and others by Nazi Germany
questions.explanation: The Holocaust (1941-1945) was the systematic genocide of 6 million Jews (and millions of others — Roma, disabled, POWs) by Nazi Germany under Adolf Hitler.

questions.question: Lord Ripon introduced:
questions.explanation: Lord Ripon (1880-1884) introduced the Local Self-Government Resolution (1882) creating elected local bodies (municipalities, district boards) with Indian participation — a step toward self-government.

questions.question: Who passed the 'Ilbert Bill' controversy?
questions.explanation: Lord Ripon's Viceroy, supported by law member Ilbert, proposed the Ilbert Bill (1883) allowing Indian judges to try Europeans. European community's protest forced major amendments.

questions.question: The term 'Panchayat' literally means:
questions.explanation: Panchayat comes from Sanskrit 'pancha' (five) + 'ayat' (council) — traditionally a council of five elders governing a village. The number 5 was considered sacred and authoritative.

questions.question: The 'Travels of Ibn Battuta' provides information about India during:
questions.explanation: Ibn Battuta (Moroccan traveler) visited India during Muhammad bin Tughluq's reign (1333-47 CE). His travelogue 'Rihla' gives detailed description of 14th century India.

questions.question: The Bhakti movement emphasised:
options: Personal devotion to God without rituals or intermediaries — devotion through love and surrender
questions.explanation: The Bhakti movement (6th–17th century) stressed personal devotion (bhakti) to God, equality before God (against caste), singing devotional songs (bhajans/kirtans), and rejection of idol worship (some saints). It was deeply democratic.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT a Tamil Bhakti saint?
questions.explanation: The Tamil Shaiva Bhakti saints (Nayanmars) include Appar, Sambandar, Sundarar, Manikkavachakar (63 Nayanmars). Kabir (15th century) was a North Indian Bhakti saint (Varanasi), not a Tamil saint.

questions.question: The 12 Alvars were Tamil Vaishnava Bhakti saints. Their devotional hymns are compiled in:
questions.explanation: The Nalayira Divya Prabandham (4000 sacred verses) compiled the hymns of the 12 Alvars. Nathamuni (10th century) collected and compiled them. Famous Alvars: Nammalvar, Andal (only female Alvar), Tirumangai Alvar.

questions.question: The Tevaram is a collection of hymns by:
options: The first three Nayanmars: Appar, Sambandar, Sundarar
questions.explanation: Tevaram is the first 7 books of the Tirumurai (12-volume compilation of Shaiva bhakti hymns). Books 1-3 are by Sambandar, Books 4-6 by Appar, Book 7 by Sundarar. Compiled by Nambi Andar Nambi in the 10th century.

questions.question: Mirabai, the Bhakti poetess, was devoted to:
questions.explanation: Mirabai (1498–1547), a Rajput princess, was a devoted saint of Lord Krishna. She composed numerous bhajans (devotional songs) and rejected her royal status for her devotion to Krishna. She is said to have merged with Krishna's image at Dwarka.

questions.question: The Sufi order 'Chishti' was the most popular in India. Its founder in India was:
questions.explanation: Moinuddin Chishti (c. 1141–1236) introduced the Chishti Sufi order to India. His dargah at Ajmer (Rajasthan) is one of the most visited pilgrimage sites in India by people of all faiths. He emphasised love for God and service to all.

questions.question: The Ryotwari System was introduced in Madras Presidency by:
questions.explanation: The Ryotwari System was introduced by Thomas Munro in Madras Presidency (1820) and later in Bombay (Elphinstone). It settled revenue directly with individual peasant farmers (ryots), eliminating zamindari intermediaries.

questions.question: The Indian Councils Act 1861 was significant because:
options: It introduced portfolio system and allowed Indians to be nominated to councils (beginning of legislative councils)
questions.explanation: Indian Councils Act 1861: introduced the portfolio system (individual ministers in charge of departments), allowed non-official (including Indian) members to be nominated to legislative councils, and allowed Viceroy to issue ordinances.

questions.question: Swami Vivekananda's famous speech that brought global attention to Hinduism was at:
questions.explanation: Swami Vivekananda addressed the Parliament of World's Religions in Chicago on 11 September 1893, beginning with 'Sisters and brothers of America'. His speech brought Hinduism and Vedanta to global attention.

questions.question: The division of the INC into Moderates and Extremists occurred at the:
questions.explanation: The Surat Split (1907) at the Surat session: Moderates (led by Gopal Krishna Gokhale) favoured constitutional methods; Extremists (led by Bal Gangadhar Tilak) demanded Swaraj, Swadeshi, Boycott, and National Education.

questions.question: Bhagat Singh, Rajguru, and Sukhdev were executed on:
questions.explanation: Bhagat Singh, Shivaram Rajguru, and Sukhdev Thapar were hanged on 23 March 1931 at Lahore Central Jail for the killing of British police officer John Saunders. They were tried under the Lahore Conspiracy Case.

questions.question: The Lahore Resolution (1940) demanding Pakistan was passed by:
options: Muslim League under Muhammad Ali Jinnah
questions.explanation: The Lahore Resolution (23 March 1940) — also called the Pakistan Resolution — was passed by the All-India Muslim League. It demanded separate independent states for Muslim-majority areas in northwest and northeast India. Jinnah gave the Two-Nation Theory.

questions.question: 'Kodi Kaatha Kumaran' (Flag-protector Kumaran) was a martyr who:
options: Climbed a hill to plant the Indian flag and was shot dead by police in Coimbatore (1932)
questions.explanation: Tiruppur Kumaran (Kodi Kaatha Kumaran) carried the Indian National Flag during a protest in Tiruppur (1932) and was beaten by British police. Though injured, he refused to let the flag fall and died holding it. He is called the 'Flag Guardian of Tiruppur'.

questions.question: The integration of Hyderabad with India (Operation Polo/Police Action) was led by:
questions.explanation: Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel (Iron Man of India) directed Operation Polo (13–17 September 1948) — police action against the Nizam of Hyderabad who refused to join India. Major General J.N. Chaudhuri led the military action.

questions.question: The INA (Indian National Army) was organized by:
questions.explanation: Subhas Chandra Bose reorganized the INA (Azad Hind Fauj) in 1943 in Singapore. 'Jai Hind', 'Dilli Chalo' were his rallying cries. Marched from Rangoon toward Delhi.

questions.question: Bhagat Singh was hanged on:
questions.explanation: Bhagat Singh, Rajguru, and Sukhdev were hanged on 23 March 1931 by the British for the killing of J.P. Saunders (avenging Lala Lajpat Rai's death).

questions.question: V.O. Chidambaram Pillai (VOC) was associated with:
questions.explanation: V.O. Chidambaram Pillai (Kappalottiya Tamilan) founded the Swadeshi Steam Navigation Company in 1906 at Tuticorin to challenge the British India Steam Navigation Company.

questions.question: Subramania Bharati is associated with:
questions.explanation: Mahakavi Subramania Bharati (1882-1921) wrote patriotic Tamil poetry, including Desiya Geetangal (National Songs), Kuyil Pattu, and Kannan Pattu. He also edited journals like India and Vijaya.

questions.question: C. Rajagopalachari (Rajaji) was the first Indian Governor-General of India. His term was:
questions.explanation: C. Rajagopalachari (Rajaji) was the last Governor-General of India and the only Indian to hold the post (June 1948 – January 1950), succeeding Lord Mountbatten.

questions.question: The Constitution of India was adopted on:
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 26 November 1949. It came into force on 26 January 1950 (Republic Day).

questions.question: Operation Vijay (1961) was related to the liberation of:
questions.explanation: Operation Vijay (18-19 December 1961) was the Indian military operation to end Portuguese colonial rule in Goa, Daman, and Diu, integrating them into India.

questions.question: The 'Green Revolution' in India is associated with:
questions.explanation: Dr. M.S. Swaminathan is called 'Father of Green Revolution in India' for introducing high-yielding varieties of wheat and rice in the 1960s, transforming India's food production.

questions.question: The French Revolution began in:
questions.explanation: The French Revolution began in 1789 with the storming of the Bastille (14 July 1789). It overthrew the monarchy and established principles of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity.

questions.question: World War I began in:
questions.explanation: World War I began in 1914 following the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand of Austria (June 28, 1914). It ended in 1918 with the Armistice of November 11.

questions.question: The United Nations was established in:
questions.explanation: The United Nations was established on 24 October 1945, replacing the League of Nations after WWII. UN Day is celebrated on 24 October every year.

questions.question: The Russian Revolution (Bolshevik) occurred in:
questions.explanation: The October Revolution (Bolshevik Revolution) of November 7, 1917 (October 25 in old Julian calendar) led by Lenin overthrew the Provisional Government and established Soviet Russia.

questions.question: The Berlin Wall fell in:
questions.explanation: The Berlin Wall (built 1961) fell on 9 November 1989, leading to German reunification in 1990 and symbolizing the end of the Cold War.

questions.question: The Marshall Plan was a U.S. initiative to:
questions.explanation: Marshall Plan (1948-52) provided $13 billion in economic aid for rebuilding war-devastated Western European economies, preventing communist expansion.

questions.question: The Magna Carta was signed in:
questions.explanation: Magna Carta (Great Charter) was signed by King John of England in 1215 CE, limiting royal powers and establishing principles of rule of law — a foundation of constitutional democracy.

questions.question: Hiroshima was bombed on:
questions.explanation: The first atomic bomb 'Little Boy' was dropped on Hiroshima on 6 August 1945. The second 'Fat Man' was dropped on Nagasaki on 9 August 1945.

questions.question: Nelson Mandela was released from prison after:
questions.explanation: Nelson Mandela was imprisoned from 1964 to 1990 — 27 years in prison (mostly on Robben Island). He was released on 11 February 1990 and became South Africa's first Black president in 1994.

questions.question: The States Reorganisation Act (1956) reorganised Indian states based on:
questions.explanation: The States Reorganisation Act 1956 reorganised Indian states based on linguistic lines, following the Fazl Ali Commission recommendations. 14 states and 6 Union Territories were created. Andhra Pradesh (1953) was the first linguistic state, formed for Telugu speakers.

questions.question: Tamil Nadu was renamed from 'Madras State' in:
questions.explanation: Madras State was renamed 'Tamil Nadu' (Land of the Tamils) on 14 January 1969 (Thai Pongal day) by Chief Minister C.N. Annadurai (Anna). The state of Madras had been created from the Madras Presidency after independence.

questions.question: The League of Nations was established after:
options: World War I (1919, Treaty of Versailles)
questions.explanation: The League of Nations was established in 1920 under the Treaty of Versailles after WWI. Proposed by US President Woodrow Wilson (14 Points), but the US Senate rejected membership. The League failed to prevent WWII and was replaced by the UN (1945).

questions.question: Which of the following dynasties built the famous Shore Temple in Mahabalipuram?
questions.explanation: The Pallavas built the Shore Temple, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. It is a testament to their architectural prowess.

questions.question: Who was the founder of the Vijayanagara Empire?
questions.explanation: Harihara and Bukka founded the Vijayanagara Empire, but Harihara is often credited as the primary founder.

questions.question: Which South Indian kingdom was known for its powerful navy?
questions.explanation: The Cholas were known for their powerful navy, which played a significant role in their maritime trade and conquests.

questions.question: Who was the famous Tamil poet and saint of the Bhakti movement?
questions.explanation: Manikkavachagar was a famous Tamil poet and saint of the Bhakti movement, known for his devotional hymns.

questions.question: The Indus Valley Civilization is known for its sophisticated?
questions.explanation: The Indus Valley Civilization is known for its sophisticated drainage system, which was advanced for its time.

questions.question: The Vedic age is characterized by the composition of the?
questions.explanation: The Vedic age is characterized by the composition of the Vedas, which are ancient Indian texts.

questions.question: The Mauryan Empire was founded by?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya founded the Mauryan Empire, which was a major power in ancient India.

questions.question: The Gupta period is often referred to as the?
questions.explanation: The Gupta period is often referred to as the Golden Age of ancient India, due to its cultural and scientific achievements.

questions.question: The British conquest of India began with the?
questions.explanation: The British conquest of India began with the Battle of Plassey, which marked the beginning of British rule in India.

questions.question: The socio-reform movement in Tamil Nadu was led by?
questions.explanation: E.V. Ramasamy, also known as Periyar, led the socio-reform movement in Tamil Nadu, advocating for social justice and equality.

questions.question: Which of the following Indus Valley cities is located in modern-day Pakistan?
questions.explanation: Mohenjo-Daro is one of the major cities of the Indus Valley Civilization, located in modern-day Pakistan. It is known for its well-planned architecture.

questions.question: Who was the founder of the Mauryan Empire?
questions.explanation: Chandragupta Maurya was the founder of the Mauryan Empire, which was a major power in ancient India.

questions.question: Which of the following South Indian kingdoms was known for its powerful navy?
questions.explanation: The Chola kingdom was known for its powerful navy, which played a significant role in its military campaigns.

questions.question: Which of the following socio-reform movements aimed to uplift the status of women in Tamil society?
questions.explanation: The Self-Respect Movement, led by E.V. Ramasamy, aimed to uplift the status of women in Tamil society and promote social reform.

questions.question: Who was the leader of the Indian National Congress during the Non-Cooperation Movement?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi was the leader of the Indian National Congress during the Non-Cooperation Movement, which was a major campaign against British rule.

questions.question: Which of the following battles marked the beginning of the British conquest of India?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Plassey marked the beginning of the British conquest of India, as it led to the defeat of the ruler of Bengal and the establishment of British rule.

questions.question: Who was the Maratha leader who challenged the British East India Company's power in India?
questions.explanation: Shivaji was the Maratha leader who challenged the British East India Company's power in India and established a powerful Maratha kingdom.

questions.question: Which of the following Vedic age texts is considered the oldest?
questions.explanation: The Rigveda is considered the oldest of the Vedic age texts, and is a key source of information about ancient Indian society and culture.

questions.question: Who was the Pallava king who built the famous Kailasanathar Temple?
questions.explanation: Narasimhavarman was the Pallava king who built the famous Kailasanathar Temple, which is a major example of Pallava architecture.

questions.question: Which of the following Indus Valley cities is known for its planned architecture?
questions.explanation: Mohenjo-Daro is known for its planned architecture, with advanced drainage systems and well-planned streets.

questions.question: The Chola kingdom was founded by?
questions.explanation: Vijayalaya is considered the founder of the Chola kingdom.

questions.question: The Pallavas were defeated by the Chalukyas in the battle of?
questions.explanation: The Pallavas were defeated by the Chalukyas in the battle of Badami.

questions.question: The Vijayanagara Empire was founded by?
questions.explanation: Harihara and Bukka Raya founded the Vijayanagara Empire.

questions.question: The British East India Company established its first factory in India at?
questions.explanation: The British East India Company established its first factory in India at Surat.

questions.question: The Sepoy Mutiny of 1857 started at?
questions.explanation: The Sepoy Mutiny of 1857 started at Meerut.

questions.question: The Non-Cooperation Movement was launched by?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi launched the Non-Cooperation Movement.

questions.question: The Home Rule Movement in India was led by?
questions.explanation: Annie Besant led the Home Rule Movement in India.

questions.question: Which of the following Indus Valley cities is known for its sophisticated drainage system?
questions.explanation: Mohenjo-Daro is known for its advanced urban planning and drainage system. It is one of the largest Indus Valley cities.

questions.question: Who is the author of the ancient Tamil epic 'Silappatikaram'?
questions.explanation: Ilango Adigal is the author of the ancient Tamil epic 'Silappatikaram', which is one of the five great epics of Tamil literature.

questions.question: The Mauryan emperor who converted to Buddhism after the Kalinga War was:
questions.explanation: Ashoka converted to Buddhism after the Kalinga War and became a great patron of the religion.

questions.question: The Chola king who built the famous Brihadeeswara Temple at Thanjavur was:
questions.explanation: Raja Raja Chola built the famous Brihadeeswara Temple at Thanjavur, which is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

questions.question: The founder of the Vijayanagara Empire was:
questions.explanation: Harihara and Bukka Raya were the founders of the Vijayanagara Empire, but Harihara is often credited as the main founder.

questions.question: The Maratha leader who fought against the British in the Anglo-Maratha Wars was:
questions.explanation: Mahadaji Scindia was a prominent Maratha leader who fought against the British in the Anglo-Maratha Wars.

questions.question: The social reformer who founded the Brahmo Samaj was:
questions.explanation: Raja Ram Mohan Roy founded the Brahmo Samaj, a social reform movement that aimed to reform Hindu society.

questions.question: The Indian National Congress was founded in:
questions.explanation: The Indian National Congress was founded in 1885 by A.O. Hume and others.

questions.question: The Quit India Movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi in:
questions.explanation: The Quit India Movement was launched by Mahatma Gandhi in 1942, demanding immediate independence for India.

questions.question: Which of the following dynasties built the Great Stupa at Sanchi?
questions.explanation: The Great Stupa at Sanchi was built during the Maurya dynasty. It is a famous Buddhist monument in India.

questions.question: The capital of the Pallava kingdom was
questions.explanation: Kanchipuram was the capital of the Pallava kingdom, known for its ancient temples and historical significance.

questions.question: The Chola kingdom reached its peak during the reign of
questions.explanation: Raja Raja Chola was a prominent king of the Chola dynasty, known for his military conquests and administrative skills.

questions.question: The Indus Valley Civilization is known for its
questions.explanation: The Indus Valley Civilization is notable for its sophisticated urban planning, with well-organized cities and advanced drainage systems.

questions.question: The Vedic age is characterized by the composition of
questions.explanation: The Vedic age is marked by the composition of the Vedas, ancient Indian texts that form the basis of Hinduism.

questions.question: The Maratha empire was founded by
questions.explanation: Shivaji was the founder of the Maratha empire, a powerful kingdom in western India.

questions.question: The freedom struggle in Tamil Nadu was influenced by
